toy-edge-p: Is our toy just like the big machines? Almost — but not quite. Our toy counts words it has seen before. The big ones cannot do that. Here is why. Most sentences you say are brand new. Nobody ever wrote them down. So there is nothing to count! A big model — a machine that learns patterns — does a different trick. It squeezes all its reading into one small shape. Like the way you remember a story without knowing every word. That is why a model cannot show you the book a fact came from. The books are not inside it. Only the squeeze is. Strange, is it not?

Our machine counts. The real ones cannot, and the reason is odd: most sentences you will ever say have never been written down anywhere, so there is nothing to count. A large model does something different — it compresses the patterns in everything it has read, more like a blurry memory of a whole library than a filing cabinet full of it. That trick lets it guess well on sentences nobody ever wrote. But there is a surprising cost: it cannot quote its sources. The original books are not stored inside it — only the squeezed-down patterns are.

Three things are worth catching with your own hands. You just caught a machine cheating by memorising.

Run three experiments before you scroll on. First: temperature 0, run it twice — identical output, and it falls into a cycle, because greedy decoding is deterministic and a finite context set must eventually revisit a state, after which it loops forever. Second: sweep temperature from 0 to 1.2 and read the output at each stop — flat, then fresh, then noise; nobody derived the “right” value, it is an empirical dial tuned by taste. Third: memory at 3 on a 250-word corpus — fluency jumps because most three-word contexts occur exactly once, leaving only one continuation, so generation collapses into quotation. That third one is overfitting, caught red-handed.

Three behaviours here generalise straight to production systems. Greedy decoding (temperature 0) is deterministic over a finite context set, so it must revisit a state and then cycle — degenerate repetition is a decoding pathology before it is a model one. Temperature has no derivation; it is a free parameter tuned by preference, which should bother you more than it usually does. And order-3 over 250 words makes most contexts unique, so generation collapses to retrieval: memorisation presenting as fluency. A question to carry out of the toy: when any system sounds this fluent, is it generalising — or was the context so specific that only one continuation existed to copy?
toy-bridge: Ready for the big secret of this whole page? The chatty machines on phones work like your toy. Guess a word. Keep it. Guess again. Round and round. But they cannot count the way your toy counts. People make up new sentences all day long. There are far too many to count. So a large language model — an LLM, a machine that guesses words — learns the squeeze trick instead. It has read more than you could read in a thousand lifetimes. That is why it sounds so smooth. And when it is wrong? It is wrong for the same reason your toy is. Guessing made it smooth. Guessing made it wrong too.

dream-lawyer: Here is a true story. A lawyer asked a chatbot to find real court cases for him. It made them up — every one — but they sounded real. He got in big trouble. “I did not know it could do that,” he said. Was the machine broken? No. Making things up is just how guessing works.

A real lawyer learnt this the hard way. It was running exactly as built — the same guess-the-next-word trick you just watched, wearing a lawyer’s vocabulary.

People in your decade learnt this the expensive way. where the single axis breaks down
dial-edge-p: Did you spot the trick? This one line squashes three different knobs into one. One knob: how wild the guesses get. One knob: may the machine look things up first? One knob: did it learn to say “I don’t know”? Builders turn each knob on its own. A simple line is a good start — not the whole story.

One dial is really a squeeze. “How much it dreams” bundles three separate levers: how risky its guesses are (the temperature slider you played with), whether it can look things up before answering, and whether it was taught to say “I don’t know”. Engineers move each one on its own — so treat the dial as a good first map, not the whole territory.

One axis is a squeeze: “how much it dreams” really compresses at least three separate levers — how riskily it samples (your temperature slider), whether it is given lookups to ground itself (retrieval), and whether it was trained to say “I don't know” (abstention). Engineers pull them separately. The dial is a good first map, not the territory.

The axis compresses at least three orthogonal levers. Sampling risk: the temperature you set on the toy. Grounding: whether retrieval is bolted into the loop. Abstention: whether training rewarded “I don’t know”. Engineers tune them independently, and they interact — retrieval at high temperature still paraphrases its sources into fiction. One experiment while the toy is near: set temperature to 0, run twice, then 1.2 — you have moved exactly one lever and left the other two alone. A single dial is a first map, not the territory.

“How much it dreams” compresses at least three orthogonal levers — sampling entropy, retrieval grounding, trained abstention — which engineers tune independently and which interact in ways one axis cannot express. That is a real cost of this page’s design: a single dial buys placeability at the price of mechanism. And the deepest lever is the least settled: abstention training trades hallucination against usefulness, and no published marking scheme yet buys honesty without also buying refusals on questions the model could have answered. Map, not territory — here more than anywhere on this page.
rubric-lead: Now the most hopeful bit. Some of the making-things-up is about marks, not smarts. When the machine was learning, right answers got a point. Saying nothing got nothing. So what is the clever move when you are stuck? Guess, and sound sure! Change the marks below. Watch the same machine change its ways.

You have sat a test where a blank scores nothing but a lucky guess might score full marks — so you guessed. The machine sat that kind of test millions of times while it was trained: right answers scored, “I don’t know” scored nothing. Under those rules the clever move on a hard question is to guess with confidence, so that is the habit it learnt. The hopeful part: change the marking scheme below and the very same machine changes its behaviour.

Here is the strangest part, and the most hopeful: some of the making-things-up is not ability at all — it is marking. The machine was graded, during training, the way schools grade a multiple-choice test: right answers score, blanks score nothing. Under those rules, what is the clever move on a question you do not know? Guess confidently. Change the marking and watch the same machine change its behaviour.

The most actionable claim in this section: a measurable share of hallucination is incentive, not capability. Standard benchmarks score binary — a correct answer earns 1, an abstention earns 0 — so a calibrated guesser maximises expected score by bluffing on every uncertain question. That is a property of the rubric, not of the weights. Test it below: award “I don’t know” 0.5 and watch the optimal strategy flip from bluff to abstain once confidence drops below half. Same machine, different marking, different behaviour.

Part of the hallucination rate is rubric, not capability: under binary scoring a calibrated model maximises expected score by guessing whenever its confidence exceeds zero, so confident bluffing is the trained-in optimum. Rescore abstention above zero and the optimum flips — Kalai et al. (2025) make precisely this argument. What it leaves open: credit for “I don’t know” suppresses bluffing but also suppresses correct low-confidence answers, and no published rubric yet separates the two cleanly. A fair question to turn on this page, which marks nothing at all: what behaviour does that choice train in you?

agent-lead: Do you know what a tool is? A hammer is a tool. A calculator is a tool too. The toy up above can only talk. It cannot do a very hard sum, because it never read that sum anywhere. So give the toy a calculator. And give it a way to search its own page. Give it a job, and let it work step by step. Now something new is here. It is an agent — a guesser that uses tools. The agent guesses a step, uses a tool, looks, and goes again. It stops when the job is done. That is the whole idea.

You have reached for a calculator when a sum got too big for your head. The toy needs the same favour. Talking is all it can do, so it is stuck the moment talk is not enough — a hard times question like 47 × 93 would beat it. Not because it is bad at maths: it never does maths at all. It only continues text, and no page it read happens to continue those digits. So give the same guesser two tools — a calculator, and a search over its own page. Add a goal, and permission to work in steps. Something new appears: an agent, a guessing machine that runs tools over and over until the goal is met. That single sentence is the whole idea.

A guesser that can only talk is stuck the moment talk is not enough. Ask one for 47 × 93 in a single breath and it must continue the digits from pattern — and the pages it read rarely continue them correctly. But give the same guesser two tools — a calculator, and a search over its own page — plus a goal and permission to work in steps, and something new appears. An agent: a guessing machine that runs tools in a loop until a goal is met. That one sentence is the whole idea.

A model that can only emit text is stuck the moment text is not enough. The toy above would fumble 47 × 93 in one pass: nothing in its corpus continues those digits, and nothing in it computes. Give the same model two tools — a calculator, and a search over its own corpus — plus a goal and permission to act in steps. What appears is an agent: a model calling tools in a think → act → observe loop until a termination check passes. That is the entire definition. Planners, memory, sub-agents, orchestration — engineering layers over that one sentence.

A text-only model stalls the moment text stops sufficing: the toy cannot continue 47 × 93, because continuation is all it does. Add two tools — a calculator, a search over its own corpus — plus a goal and a loop, and you have an agent: a model calling tools until a termination check passes. One sentence of definition. Notice where the weight sits: not in the tools, in the predicate. Who writes the check? What counts as met? Hold those questions — the demo below is too tidy to answer them.
agent-after: What did the agent just do? It thought, used a tool, looked, and thought again. Round and round, like tidying a room one toy at a time. Did it stop? Yes — it checked the job, saw it was done, and stopped. You saw the stop happen. Now the best part. Take a tool away and the agent does not get cross. It just stops and says what it needed. You choose what it can touch. And did you see the steps help? Small steps beat one big blurt. That is true for the agent — is it true for you too?

Three things you just saw are worth keeping. First, the loop is not magic: think, use a tool, look at the result, decide again — that is all of it. Second, the loop is not unstoppable: it ends at a visible check, and you watched that check trip. Third, the loop is not free-willed: switch a tool off and the agent does not sulk or sneak — it stops and says which tool it needed. What it can touch was your setting the whole time. And here is the quiet surprise: the machine only gets a small, fixed bit of thinking per word it writes. Let it work in steps and it genuinely does better than when it must blurt one answer — you saw that with the calculator.

Three things you just saw, worth keeping. The loop is not magic: think, use a tool, look at the result, decide again. The loop is not unstoppable: the exit is a visible check, and you tripped it yourself. And the loop is not free-willed: switch a tool off and the agent does not rage — it halts and names what it lacked. What it may touch was your setting all along. Steps matter for a quieter reason too: a guesser spends a fixed sliver of effort per word, so a machine allowed to think out loud in stages genuinely does better than one forced to blurt — you watched that happen with the calculator.

Three observations worth keeping. The loop is not magic: think, call a tool, read the result, decide again — a plain control loop with a model in the decide slot. It is not unstoppable: termination is an explicit, visible check, and you tripped it yourself. And it is not free-willed: deny a tool and the agent halts and names the missing capability. Its permissions are configuration you own, not character it has. The step count matters mechanically: a model spends fixed compute per token — one forward pass. Reasoning across many tokens therefore buys more serial computation than a one-breath answer, which is why the calculator run beat the blurt. Experiment: run the same goal twice, once with both tools allowed, once with the calculator denied — compare the traces, and read exactly what the halt message names.

Keep three negations. Not magic: a control loop with a model in the decide slot. Not unstoppable: termination is an authored, visible check. Not free-willed: deny a tool and it halts, naming the gap — permissions are configuration. The step effect is mechanical: fixed compute per token, so more reasoning tokens buy more serial computation — the observation behind test-time scaling. Now the questions this demo is too small to face. Its exit check is hand-written and always legible; real termination predicates are neither — who audits “goal met”? Its thought lines match its actions; in the wild, do visible reasoning traces cause the behaviour, or decorate it? And “what it may touch was your setting” — does that comfort survive an agent composing tools you allowed into effects you did not?

fork-lead: Do you know what a fork in the road is? It is the spot where one road splits into two. A long, long time ago, the people building clever machines stood at a fork like that. One group asked: can we make a machine smart on its own? That idea is called artificial intelligence. The other group asked a sneaky question: can a machine make people smarter? That idea is called augmentation — help that makes you more able. Both roads got built. The guessing machine you just met came from the first road. The second road gave you things you touch every day, like the mouse on a computer. You have been using the second road your whole life. Can you spot one of its gifts near you right now?

Everything you have read so far — the guessers, the agents — comes from one side of an old split. Around 1960, two teams asked two different questions. John McCarthy’s team asked: can we make machines intelligent? Doug Engelbart’s team asked a sneakier one: can machines make people more capable? The first idea is called artificial intelligence; the second, intelligence augmentation. Here is the surprise: both worked. You have been reading about the first. The second built the computer mouse, the clickable screen, the tools you have been using your whole life — you just never heard its name.

McCarthy’s camp asked: can we make machines intelligent? Engelbart’s camp, at SRI, asked: can machines make people more capable? AI versus IA — artificial intelligence versus intelligence augmentation. Both shipped, and the second arguably shipped first: the mouse, hypertext and the windowed screen all came out of Engelbart’s lab by 1968. Around 1960 the field split — McCarthy asking whether machines could be intelligent, Engelbart whether machines could make people more capable. AI and IA. Both shipped; IA shipped first, then vanished into the furniture — mouse, hypertext, windows — which is why you have spent your life using it without a name for it. Worth asking why this guide spends six sections on AI before naming the branch you actually live in. Partly structure; partly, perhaps, the same attention gravity that starved IA of funding for decades. That is IA’s core open problem: augmentation that works stops being visible as augmentation. Hold the question.
engel-def: Quick question: are you better at sums with a pencil and paper, or with nothing at all? A man called Doug Engelbart thought hard about that. He said the smart one was never just the person alone. It is the person plus their words, their tools and their ways of doing things — all working as one team. He wanted to augment people, not replace them. Augment means help you do more. Make the pencil better, and the whole team gets smarter. Make the words better — same thing. So here is the check: what is in your team? You, and what else?

You already know a version of this: try doing long division in your head, then with pen and paper. The paper is doing part of the thinking. Doug Engelbart wrote the founding paper on this idea in the early 1960s, and his goal was not a smarter machine — it was a more capable you. His sharpest thought is the surprising bit: the smart unit was never the person alone. It is the person plus their tools, their methods, and even their language — all trained together as one system. Words count as tools too. Upgrade any part of that system, and the whole person gets smarter at hard problems.

Engelbart wrote the founding paper in 1962 and defined the goal precisely: increasing a person's capability “to approach a complex problem situation, to gain comprehension to suit his particular needs, and to derive solutions to problems.” Not a smarter machine — a more capable you. His sharpest insight was that the smart unit was never the person alone: it is the person plus their language, their tools and their methods, trained together as one system. Upgrade any part and the whole person-system gets smarter.

The founding paper is Engelbart’s 1962 “Augmenting Human Intellect: A Conceptual Framework”. It defines the goal precisely: increasing a person’s capability “to approach a complex problem situation, to gain comprehension to suit his particular needs, and to derive solutions to problems.” Not a smarter machine — a more capable you. The load-bearing claim is the unit of analysis. Not the human alone but the H-LAM/T system: the human plus language, artifacts and methodology, with the training that binds them into one system. Upgrade any component and the whole system’s capability moves. Try it as an experiment: list your own stack — editor, search, note method, prompt habits — then deliberately downgrade one for an hour and log what breaks.

Engelbart, 1962: capability “to approach a complex problem situation, to gain comprehension to suit his particular needs, and to derive solutions to problems.” The unit of analysis is the real claim: not the person but the person plus language, artifacts and methods, trained as one system — H-LAM/T. Two consequences remain underpriced. Co-evolution: upgraded tools retrain the human, so the system is a moving target — we can benchmark a model, but who benchmarks a person-system? And bootstrapping: using the augmented system to improve the augmenting itself, the loop this site is quietly running on you. Fair to ask whether that loop is guidance or capture. Engelbart never fully settled it either.
engel-brick: Engelbart showed this with a funny trick. Imagine tying a heavy brick to your pencil. Now try to write your name. It goes slow and wobbly, and your arm aches. See what that shows? A lot of your good writing was living in the pencil, not just in you. Try it yourself — no real brick needed:

He proved the idea backwards, with a brick. Strap a brick to a pencil and try writing: it turns slow, wobbly and exhausting within seconds. The surprise is what that reveals — a big share of “being good at writing” was living in the pencil all along, and you only notice when it is taken away. Feel it yourself:

His demonstration ran in reverse: de-augmentation. Strap a brick to a pencil and writing degrades at once — slower, wobblier, costlier per word. The measurement matters more than the stunt: you can quantify an augmentation by what its removal costs. Run the demo below and watch your own curve. The proof runs backwards: de-augmentation. Brick on pencil, writing degrades, and the skill turns out to have lived partly in the tool. Methodologically it is the cleanest move in the 1962 paper — measure augmentation by ablation, the same trick we now run on models. It cuts the other way too: if the pencil holds part of “good at writing”, what does the chatbot hold, and what is left as yours? The slider below stages that question on your own hand:

spectrum-lead: Do you know what a team-mate is? Now look at your tools. Some tools only move when you move them. That kind is called an extension — a longer arm for you. Some tools add help you could not make on your own. That kind is called a partner — like the fig tree and the fig wasp, two friends who each do what the other cannot. And some tools do the whole job while you walk away. That kind is called a replacement. Where do your tools sit on that line? Drag them and pick a spot. There is no wrong answer here. You have seen your phone finish a sentence for you — so who was writing, you or it? Licklider drew a line for exactly that question. At one end sits the extension: it does only what you steer, stroke by stroke, like a pencil. In the middle sits the partner: it adds something you could not do alone, the way the fig tree and the fig wasp each do what the other cannot. At the far end sits the replacement: it does the whole job while you leave the room. Drag today's tools onto the line wherever you think they belong. And here is the odd part: there is no answer key — people in my time still argue about it at dinner.

ar-lead: Have you ever pointed a phone at a flower to ask its name? That is augmented reality — helpful words on real things. The phone looks with you, then puts a little note on top of what you see.

You have probably used this branch already without knowing its name. Point your phone camera at a plant, a menu or a sign in another language, and useful facts appear on top of what you see — that is augmented reality. One visual search tool alone does this billions of times a month; the surprise is that even its owner cannot count it the same way twice.

ar-glasses: Now there are glasses that can show words right in front of your eyes. A band on your wrist feels your muscles start to move, so the glasses know what you want. They can write down what people say, so a friend who cannot hear can read it. The dream glasses from films are real too — but they are not in any shop yet — no one can buy them, not even with lots and lots of money.

Glasses with a tiny screen in one lens went on sale in your decade for about $800. They come with a wristband that reads the small electrical signals your muscles make, so the glasses respond almost before your fingers move. That means captions for deaf people, translations for travellers and directions for the lost, floating on the world itself. The full sci-fi pair exists too — and here is the odd part: it cost about $10,000 to build, and no amount of money could buy one, because the hard problems of your decade were price, weight and battery, never what the glasses could do.

The glasses arrived in your decade too. From September 2025 you could buy a $799 pair with a small display in one lens — and a wristband that steers it by reading the tiny electrical signals your muscles make before your fingers move. Captions for the deaf, translations for the traveller, directions for the lost, floating on the world itself. And the sci-fi pair — wide field of view, the whole movie dream? It exists. It cost about $10,000 a unit to build, and you could not buy it at any price. The frontier of your decade was never capability. It was price, weight and battery.

The glasses shipped in your decade. From September 2025, $799 bought a monocular display plus an EMG wristband — surface electrodes reading the motor signals your muscles fire milliseconds before your fingers visibly move. Day-one uses: live captions, translation, navigation overlays pinned to the world. The wide-field-of-view prototype existed as well, at roughly $10,000 per unit to build and unsellable at any price. Read that as an engineering claim: the binding constraints were price, weight and battery — capability was not the frontier.
